item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked it I think it was a cool premise and a nice touch seeing the GRU-P not being the Soviet governments bitch but the exploration were a little confusing but still +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
tl;dr - "I have been falling, for THIRTY MINUTES!"

Was a huge fan of this article before I made my account, and finally found it again to give it the +1 it deserves.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've seen many instances of "GoC screwing up" it's nice to see one time when they actually save the day while the foundation is content with letting everything happen. Upvoted.

I'm really quite proud of this article, and grateful to everyone who helped me write it. I'm only sorry that I didn't finish Guns Pointed at the Head of God earlier. But it will return — this year. Stay tuned!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No problem!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks, I'll keep that in mind.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Gulag - A camp in the Gulag system, or any political labor camp."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why is "gulag" consistently being used as a synonym to "camp"? It's an abberviation, translated as "State directorate of camps" or some such. Makes as much sense as saying "fbi" while intending to say "Guantanamo bay".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very strange, but a good skip nonetheless. The dialogue felt real and that GOC cameo was nice. +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was just thinking that GOC should be right at least some of the time, so I'm going to disagree with the above posters and be happy to have a SCiP where GOC solves a problem for the Foundation.
Fantastic! I've always considered this one of my best articles and I hope to have Season 2 of Guns Pointed out by the end of the year!

item.title: CROSS CANON AHOY
item.content:encoded: With the blessing of Kalinin, SCP-2664 has officially become the newest entrant into The Coldest War! Please note that the rest of the Guns Pointed series is not part of The Coldest War; they continue to be firmly within the wheelhouse of Third Law. Furthermore, SCP-2664 will also continue to be a part of Third Law, making it the third ever cross-canon article after SCP-2987 and SCP-2350.

item.title: Re: First
item.content:encoded:
I can only imagine if kaktus actually called me to give crit.

Phone rings

Me: Hello?

Kaktus: First of all, you don't need to have a comma every two words…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted, though I found the logs quite confusing near the middle (especially when people other than 8 and 7 start talking), echoing a few other people I guess.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Personal theory: you can't kill something that wasn't alive to begin with. I think the GOC done made their biggest screw-up yet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This. As I said to ARD in chat, the exploration logs were good and helped create the twist at the end. The GOC killing it just destroys that sense of horror that this article created, being one of the first articles in a long time to make me go 'oh shit.'

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Unfortunately I'm gonna have to neutral vote
I really like the scp and I wanted to know more about the triplets, and then it just ended
After all the exploration logs it turns out the GOC just fire some unknown weapon at the dome and it disappears, felt like a bit of a cop out
Hell yeah. Just in time for the launch of Guns Pointed at the Head of God.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Neutral-voting this. The idea of a pacifist GRU-P is interesting, and I appreciate that you didn't dwell too much on the hypocrisy of the group itself, because that's obvious and not something I need spelled out for me. I found the interview logs to be the strongest part of the article.

The exploration logs didn't really do it for me—it was a mixed bag of Akira-esque images, some of which worked, some of which didn't. I think you could've gotten to the point of "it's all happening in the thing's head" a bit more quickly. I think the idea of assimilation that you end on is interesting, but not something that was really developed that much in the logs, and so not really something that impacted me.

item.title: Re: ALERT: IMPENDING YK-CLASS END-OF-WORLD SCENARIO
item.content:encoded: All I know in life has become hype.

item.title: ALERT: IMPENDING YK-CLASS END-OF-WORLD SCENARIO
item.content:encoded: WARNING: At this time, SCP-2664 is growing at an abnormally rapid rate. All attempts to retard its growth have failed. It is predicted that SCP-2664 will reach populated areas within forty-eight hours. Overwatch Command has been informed of this event. Stay tuned over the next three days for more information.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It was good until the exploration logs, it's just a not-particularly-interesting tale shoe-horned into a SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
LOST TO THE ZONE

I'm going to echo the other comments - I love the backstory behind their attempt to make a weapon for peace, the body horror, the psychic elements, and the exploration log reveals. +1 from me.

*А ну, чики-брики и в дамки!*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmmmm… I'm a little torn on this.

+ show block
– hide block

I love the politicking, internal GRU-P, Soviet national, and international alike. The idea of these folks frantically shoving together theory in an applied setting is pretty neat, and I like the test descriptions and context.

On the downside, you've got some conservation of narrative issues. There's a lot of content that's repeated between the description, interview, and logs that makes the latter parts less interesting to read. In contrast, there were other things that could've used some development, or at least felt kinda perfunctory compared to the amount of text spent repeating the meat and potatoes (e.g., the project development steps in the interview feel a little tossed-off/could use some show-don't-tell, compared to the text length. They'd probably be fine otherwise).

Also, I enjoyed a lot about the exploration logs, but I just couldn't buy the whole 'walk into the bunker, end up literally transported into a sparse psionic midnspace' thing. It's just a little too… Statuary morning cartoon for me. When I'm already chewing on that, pushing into imagery of them being chased by a ghost while reciting dialogue form traumatic events goes well into narm territory.

Edit: Actually, there's a fair amount of show-don't-tell problems here, e.g., "Oh no the monster's going to eat me I only have time to spell out to you the reader exactly what's happening, which you probably didn't pick up from the random weird imagery we've been seeing." (Note: I don't have much problems with said random weird events OR the story exposited, just commenting on the distribution.) This was really less of a problem than the other two points.

So, no vote. There's a lot to love here, but it doesn't quite mesh for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What the fuck, ARD.

I love it. Take your upvote and deliver more of this weird shit directly to my face (if you want to and feel capable of doing so of course, this is a polite request and not a demand).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That was pretty damn good - while the "it's getting bigger" ending is a bit old hat for me, the brain-stretching (both figurative and literal) story was well-paced and disturbing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love mind control experiments in the cold war. Something about Nazi or Russian experiment stories always get me.

That and I'm always a sucker for exploration logs. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Like this version. Good work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Made that change, thank you!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I noted a bug:

I was a Project Manager in the Psychotronics Division of the Main Intelligence Agency of the General Staff of the Armed Forces of the Russian Federation.

When I saw this beast of a title I was a bit put off by it. Initially I wanted to suggest breaking it up with commas, then I found that it was just not the length that is off, it is the title itself. Psychotronics Division of the… Russian Federation?

Also, I found it strange that the GRU was named an Agency because I remember that the Soviet GRU was always referred to as a Directorate. Then I looked it up on Wikipedia and it all dawned on me. You copied the name from the wiki page on the modern GRU, do you?

Perhaps it would feel better if you just rephrase it into:

I was a project manager in the Psychotronics Division, Main Intelligence Directorate.

The general staff bit can be trimmed away because I assume that iceman's captors already knew GRU's position in the Soviet military hierarchy.

Otherwise, it's a good skip!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm afraid the exploration logs kind of lost me, what with all of the members of the task force seeming to psychically communicate using the two involved. The rest of it is good, but I'm afraid the confusing nature of the logs pushes me into a no-vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was phenomenal. The wonderfully ominous SCProcedures immediately reeled me in, and set the tone for the article in the right way. There's a lot to love throughout; psychic MTF force (and the little bits of world-building surrounding psionic warfare), the rouge GRU-P dooming the world trying to save it, the exploration log…

Damn, that exploration log. I do love me some exploration logs. Here, it's expertly crafted. It's the right mix of weird and wrong, and the way you tell it was brilliant.

Very solid. A must read. Enthusiastic +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fantastic. Gruesome. Uncomfortable. Confusing, but that's the nature of it. Great logs, although I find the language used between the squad a TINY bit unconvincing- they were very casual when faced with such an extreme situation.

A well deserved +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The one time where we really need Telekill…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Admittedly, you couldn't tell this story the same way without having someone communicating telepathically through someone else, and that ultimately justifies using it.

As for the metal, that's funny, because I kept thinking "What about Telekill?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why the references to orichalcum and electrum?

There's a couple stories on the site that connect orichalcum to that much maligned metal Telekill. I wanted to maintain the idea that orichalcum messes with psionic abilities without saddling it with Telekill's history. I also mentioned electrum because I headcanon that it's a psionically dampening material like orichalcum, but without the side effects and thus more suitable for wearable protection; kind of like tin vs lead solder.

And why would the Foundation ever employ people with psionic abilities? I mean, they should be locked up and studied, yeah?

I mean, sure there's probably a more than few dangerous psychics whom the Foundation and other GoIs have incarcerated, but on the whole, this version of the Foundation believes that saying "Here, we'll pay you money to use your mind-reading, telekinetic, wireless communicating, people-influencing powers for the good of mankind" is easier and much more cost-effective than abducting people from their homes in the middle of the night. Plus, it's much more interesting and enriching from a storytelling perspective to have a Psionics Division in the Foundation - it opens up the door to all kinds of psychic escapades.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Unsettling, dangerous, out-and-out weird, everything a skip should be. Upvoted primarily for the good exploration logs.

Two questions: Why the references to orichalcum and electrum? And why would the Foundation ever employ people with psionic abilities? I mean, they should be locked up and studied, yeah?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow, this is already a fave skip for me. Great job, I'm very much looking forward to reading more in this series.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Here's another one for the GoIs that should be subordinate to their government, but decide to do things their way. Interesting interpretation of GRU-P, which adds more flavour than being an appendage to the Soviet imperial machine.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is super fucked up, and I normally think I wouldn't enjoy something like this. But I really do, it's really great. I think it's the entire angle of the pacifist Gru-P or something but this was fucked up good, dude. I hope you win the thing, imo. So far.

item.title: Re: First
item.content:encoded: Have an upvote, you butt. =^,^=

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good thing this anomaly is contained in Siberia, cause its so cool.

In all seriousness I'm glad to see it posted.

I like the sneakiness of GRU P in essentially reprogramming Stalin's propaganda machine into an antiwar vehicle, and their subsequent use of said anomaly to prevent the Cuban Missile Crisis is a creative bit of tying this into the political climate of the era.

Additionally, the exploration logs are fun, and really amp up the tension to what is, in my opinion, a very satisfying revelation at the end.

+1 ARD. Good show.

item.title: First
item.content:encoded:
This is an SCP of firsts for me. First SCP of the new year, first contest entry of the year, first SCP in four months, first exploration log SCP, and first GRU Division "P" SCP.

This is also part 0 of 1. Be sure to check out the sequel: T Minus.

I owe a huge debt to the boys in blue and silver: GreenWolf, Jacob Conwell, Taffeta TyGently, TwistedGears, Roget, and Apoplexic for reading, re-reading, and then re-re-reading it and helping me nail down stuff like tone, pacing, dialogue, and elements of style.

I owe a major debt of gratitude to djkaktus - he actually took the time to speak with me over the phone and help me make this the best it could be.

Many thanks to LurkD, weizhong, and Vezaz for draft review.

See more of my work on my author page!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In Soviet Russia, anomaly contains you.
